title: Single Motherhood and the Anger Nobody Will Help With
dek: *The fury you feel when you're parenting alone isn't a character flaw or a sign you're doing it wrong. It's your nervous system responding accurately to an impossible workload, and learning to work with that anger rather than against it might be the most practical thing you do this year.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Single mothers face structural anger that emerges not from personal failure but from carrying responsibility society promised to share. This rage is healthy data pointing toward unmet needs, yet isolation compounds when nobody validates it as rational. Working with the body's anger signals rather than suppressing them creates sustainable change without additional support systems.

Your anger knows something the parenting books won't tell you: one person cannot do the work of a village, no matter how organized her calendar or how positive her affirmations. The rage that surfaces when you're microwaving yesterday's coffee for the third time while fielding a work email and negotiating screen time isn't dysfunction. It's accurate biological feedback about a system designed for multiple adults that's now running on one. In our experience working with single mothers, the most damaging myth isn't that anger is unladylike or that good mothers don't get mad. It's the quiet belief that if you were just better at this, you wouldn't feel so furious so often. That belief keeps you aimed at the wrong target, trying to fix your emotional regulation when what actually needs fixing is a care structure built for two incomes, four hands, and the ability to tag out. ## The Structural Weight That Looks Like Personal Failure You wake at 5:47 AM not to an alarm but to the weight of your daughter's foot against your ribcage. She's climbed into your bed again, which means the morning routine just compressed by twelve minutes. You're calculating before your eyes open: if you skip the shower, you can make her a hot breakfast. If you make her eat the granola bar in the car, you can answer those emails before the actual workday starts. Every choice is a triage decision, and your body is tracking the cost. Robert Augustus Masters points out in his work on emotional intimacy that anger often arises at the boundary between what we can give and what's being asked of us. For single mothers, that boundary isn't occasionally crossed during hard weeks. It's the permanent condition of your daily architecture. You're not failing to set healthy limits. You're living in a situation where healthy limits would mean your child doesn't eat dinner or you lose your job. The anger that surfaces isn't irrational. It's your system trying to alert you that something is wrong, except there's nowhere to route that alert. There's no second parent to tap in, no extended family down the street, no structural support that matches the 1950s fantasy the school calendar still assumes. So the anger just sits in your shoulders, your jaw, the back of your skull where tension builds every afternoon around 4 PM. Over the years I've seen how this becomes a secondary problem: you start managing the anger itself as though it's the issue, rather than recognizing it as accurate feedback about an untenable situation. You download meditation apps, try breathing exercises in the Target parking lot, feel guilty when you snap at your kid over spilled juice. All that effort goes toward suppressing a signal that's trying to tell you the truth. ## What Your Body Already Knows About Impossible Loads Your nervous system doesn't read parenting blogs. It doesn't care about growth mindset or the power of positive thinking. It's tracking threat, resource availability, and whether you're safe. And when you're doing bedtime alone for the 450th night in a row after a full day of work, your body knows that you are not safe in the way humans are wired to expect safety. You're operating without backup in a high-stakes situation, and your sympathetic nervous system is responding exactly as it should. Pat Ogden's somatic psychology research emphasizes that the body holds stress in posture, breath, and muscular tension long before the thinking mind registers overwhelm. You might notice your shoulders riding up toward your ears while you're packing lunches, or the way your breath gets shallow when you're trying to stretch the grocery budget through another week. These aren't separate from your anger. They're part of the same systemic response to chronic undersupport. The specific sensation of single-parent anger often lodges in the upper back and neck, that band of muscles that tightens when you're carrying too much for too long. It's the body's way of bracing against a load it knows is too heavy, the same pattern you'd see in someone carrying buckets of water uphill without rest. Except you can't set the buckets down. They're your actual life, your actual child, your actual bills. When you feel that tightness, that's not a cue that you need to relax harder. It's information. Your system is telling you it's in a chronic state of hypervigilance because the situation genuinely requires hypervigilance. You can't forget the permission slip or miss the payment deadline or zone out during the fever check. There's no net. The anger is partly your body's protest against that relentless level of activation. ## The Isolation That Compounds When Nobody Says "Of Course You're Angry" Here's what makes it worse: the loneliness of having rational anger that everyone around you treats as a personal problem you need to fix. Your married friends suggest yoga. Your mother implies you're too stressed. Your ex's family thinks you're bitter. Nobody stands next to you and says the simple true thing: of course you're furious, anyone would be furious, this situation is legitimately infuriating. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches that anger needs to be held with the same tenderness we'd offer a crying child. But before you can hold it tenderly, someone has to acknowledge it's real. When your anger gets pathologized as your inability to cope rather than recognized as a sane response to an insane workload, you end up in a double bind. You're angry about the situation and then angry about being treated as though your anger is the situation. This is where many single mothers end up in what looks like depression but is actually suppressed rage plus exhaustion. You stop expressing the anger because nobody validates it anyway. You stop expecting help because asking just confirms how alone you are. The fury goes underground, and what's visible on the surface is a flattened affect, a tiredness that sleep doesn't touch, a sense that you're going through motions in a life that doesn't quite feel like yours. In my own practice, the turning point for many women isn't learning better coping strategies. It's hearing someone else name the structural problem out loud. It's permission to stop treating their anger as a character defect and start treating it as reasonable feedback about unreasonable conditions. That shift doesn't make the conditions easier, but it stops the secondary suffering of believing you're broken for responding normally to brokenness. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Anger That Has Nowhere to Go You can't fix the structural problem in five minutes, but you can change your relationship with the anger that's living in your body right now. This practice doesn't require childcare, equipment, or a quiet room. You can do it in your car before you go into the grocery store or in the bathroom while your kid watches TV. Sit or stand. Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Take three breaths and just notice where the anger is sitting in your body right now. Don't try to change it. Just locate it. Is it in your throat? Your shoulders? Your stomach? Your jaw? Now say out loud, even in a whisper: "This anger makes sense." You don't have to believe it yet. Just say it. "This anger makes sense given what I'm carrying." Let your hands stay where they are. Take three more breaths. Notice if anything shifts, softens, or intensifies. You're not trying to make the anger go away. You're trying to stop fighting it, just for these few breaths. If tears come, let them. If you feel more angry, that's fine too. The point isn't to feel better immediately. The point is to practice being with your anger as information rather than as evidence of your failure. You're building a new muscle: the capacity to let your anger exist without needing to fix it, suppress it, or apologize for it. ## When the Anger Is Toward Your Child (And Nobody Lets You Say It) The most unspeakable part is often the hardest part: sometimes you're angry at your kid. Not at the situation, not at your ex, not at society's failure to support parents. At the actual small person who just spilled an entire cup of milk after you explicitly said to use two hands, or who's melting down about socks for the third morning this week when you're already late. This anger feels especially shameful because your child didn't ask for this either. They didn't choose to have one parent doing the work of two. They're just being a kid, having kid-sized problems that need adult-sized solutions, and you're the only adult in the room. Again. The anger shows up anyway, and then you're angry at yourself for being angry at them, and the whole thing becomes a spiral of guilt and rage and exhaustion. But here's what's actually happening: you're angry at the situation, and your child is the most present reminder of the situation. They're the reason you can't just quit and move to a cabin. They're the reason you can't sleep in, can't make decisions based only on your own needs, can't opt out when it's hard. That's not their fault, but they're the face of the constraint, and your nervous system doesn't always distinguish between "the person creating the problem" and "the person who is present when the problem exists." If you can name this dynamic, you can work with it differently. When you feel anger rising toward your child, you can sometimes catch it and redirect: "I'm not actually angry at you. I'm angry that I'm doing this alone and you need something I don't have the bandwidth to give right now." Sometimes saying that out loud to yourself, or even to your kid in age-appropriate language, releases the charge. You're not a bad parent for feeling angry at your child sometimes. You're a human being in an impossible situation, and your anger is leaking toward the nearest target because it has to go somewhere. The [Banyan Botanicals Brahmi Oil (Sesame Base)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0D79SNVBL?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be part of a small evening ritual when the anger has been too much that day, a way to touch your own head with gentleness after hours of being everything to everyone. ## The Practical Tools That Actually Help (Without More Time or Money) You don't need another self-care prescription that requires resources you don't have. You need tactics that work within the constraints of your actual life, which means they have to be fast, free, and doable without childcare. Start tracking your anger patterns in a notes app on your phone. Just one sentence a day: "Angry at 4 PM when I realized I forgot to thaw dinner" or "Furious during bedtime, she wouldn't stay in bed." You're not journaling. You're collecting data. After two weeks, you'll see patterns. The anger isn't random. It clusters around specific times, specific transitions, specific kinds of demands. Once you see the pattern, you can sometimes do tiny preemptive things: keep frozen pizzas for the nights you forget to plan, move bedtime fifteen minutes earlier on days when you're already depleted. Learn the difference between anger that needs expression and anger that needs acknowledgment. Expression anger wants to move: yelling in your car, hitting a pillow, going for a hard walk. Acknowledgment anger just wants to be seen: writing it down, saying it out loud to yourself, texting a friend "I am so angry right now I could scream." In my own practice, I've found that mismatching the response makes it worse. If you try to journal through anger that needs to move, you'll just feel more trapped. If you try to run off anger that needs witnessing, you'll just be angry and tired. The assessments hub on this site has a simple anger-tracking tool that takes ninety seconds and helps you identify whether your anger is primarily about boundaries, exhaustion, or unprocessed grief. Knowing which kind you're working with changes how you respond to it. Build one non-negotiable micro-boundary. Not a big self-care overhaul. One tiny thing that's yours. Maybe it's the first sip of coffee before you talk to anyone. Maybe it's sitting in your car for three minutes before you go inside after work. Maybe it's saying "I need five minutes" and locking the bathroom door even if your kid is whining outside it. This isn't about the five minutes fixing anything. It's about practicing the motion of claiming space for yourself, which your anger is partially about: you have disappeared under the weight of everyone else's needs, and your rage is trying to remind you that you still exist. Some women find that starting the day with [Four Sigmatic Organic Focus Mushroom Ground Coffee | Dark Roast](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0756D1D39?tag=spankyspinola-20) creates a small ritual that feels like choosing something for yourself before the demands begin, though the real value is in the pause itself, not the beverage. ## What Changes When You Stop Trying to Be Less Angry The counterintuitive truth is that working with your anger rather than against it often reduces the destructive charge faster than any amount of suppression. When you stop spending energy trying not to be angry, that energy becomes available for other things. When you stop treating your anger as a problem to solve, it can start functioning as the information system it actually is. This doesn't mean indulging every angry impulse or yelling at your kid whenever you feel like it. It means letting yourself know what you know. It means saying to yourself, "I am furious right now, and that makes complete sense" instead of "I shouldn't feel this way, what's wrong with me." That shift in self-talk sounds small, but it's the difference between adding shame to anger and just having anger. You might notice that once you stop fighting the anger, it moves through faster. It spikes, you acknowledge it, it subsides. When you're constantly trying to suppress it, it's like holding a beach ball underwater: it takes constant effort, and the moment you get tired, it explodes to the surface anyway. Letting it exist doesn't mean it controls you. It means you're not wasting energy on a losing battle. For some women, working with the fire toolkit on this site provides a framework for this kind of shift, moving from "anger as enemy" to "anger as ally carrying important information." The tools there aren't about becoming calmer. They're about becoming more honest with yourself about what you're actually experiencing. ## The Questions Nobody Answers Honestly ### Isn't it bad for my kid to see me angry? It's worse for your kid to absorb the message that anger doesn't exist or that adults pretend to be fine when they're falling apart. What damages children isn't seeing a parent angry. It's seeing a parent unable to regulate anger, or a parent who directs rage at the child as though the child is responsible for adult problems. If you can say "I'm feeling really angry right now, and I need a few minutes to calm down, and this isn't your fault," you're modeling emotional honesty and self-regulation. That's actually protective. The fantasy that good parents never feel angry in front of their kids is both impossible and unhelpful. ### What if my anger is actually about my ex and I'm taking it out on everything else? Maybe it is. Unprocessed anger toward an ex-partner is incredibly common and often completely justified. But even if that's the source, the anger is still showing up in your body now, affecting your present-moment life. You can work with it somatically and practically even while you're also working on the deeper relational wound. They're not mutually exclusive. And sometimes the anger that looks like it's about your ex is actually about the loss of the partnership you thought you'd have, the grief of doing it alone, the betrayal of broken promises. That's layered, and it doesn't resolve quickly, but you can still learn to be with it without letting it run your life. ### How do I know if my anger is crossing into something that needs professional help? If your anger is leading to actions you regret regularly, if you're scaring yourself or your child, if you're having intrusive thoughts about harming yourself or others, if the anger is paired with substance use or complete inability to function, those are signs to reach out. The NIMH page on emotional regulation has good baseline information about when distress crosses into clinical territory. But feeling intense anger frequently in response to genuinely difficult circumstances isn't automatically pathological. The question is whether you have any capacity to be with the anger without it immediately becoming destructive behavior. If you have even a sliver of space between feeling and acting, you can build on that. ## The Deeper Work Nobody Wants to Hear About At some point, if you're doing this work honestly, you hit the layer underneath the anger about logistics and schedules and money. You hit the grief. The anger is often guarding something more vulnerable: the sadness that this isn't what you imagined, the loneliness of carrying it alone, the fear that you're not enough, the bone-deep exhaustion of never getting to rest. That grief doesn't have a solution. You can't fix it by being better organized or more patient. It's just the cost of the situation, and it needs to be felt. Not all at once, not in a way that incapacitates you, but in small doses when you have the capacity. Sometimes the anger softens when you let yourself cry about what you've lost, what your kid has lost, what you're all navigating that you never chose. This is where practices like the [Banyan Botanicals Triphala Guggulu](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Q48DGS?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support the physical body during emotional processing, helping with the somatic dimension of releasing what's been held, though the real work is in allowing the feelings themselves. The other piece of deeper work is recognizing that you're not just trying to survive until it gets easier. You're building a self that can hold complexity, that can be angry and tender at the same time, that can acknowledge impossibility while still showing up. That's not a consolation prize. That's actually a kind of integrity that people in easier circumstances don't always develop. It doesn't make the situation fair, but it does mean something is being forged in you that matters. ## What's on the Other Side of Pretending You're Fine You don't arrive at a place where you're never angry. The conditions of single parenthood don't magically resolve because you've done your inner work. But you can arrive at a place where the anger doesn't scare you, where you know how to be with it, where you've stopped treating it as evidence that you're failing. You might find yourself able to say to your kid, "I'm really frustrated right now, and I'm going to take five minutes in my room, and then we'll figure this out." You might find yourself able to feel the anger spike and not immediately spiral into shame about feeling it. You might find yourself able to ask for help without the request being wrapped in apology and explanation. Small shifts, but they're the shifts that make the daily weight more bearable. The work we've been exploring here isn't about transcending your anger or becoming some sort of enlightened single parent who floats above the hard parts. It's about coming into right relationship with the reality of what you're living, which includes the reality of your anger. And that right relationship might be the most radical thing you do, in a culture that wants you to smile through it and never admit how hard it actually is. You can learn more about this kind of honest, body-based approach to difficult emotions at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) and through the ongoing work at the about page, where we talk more about why this perspective matters. The anger you carry isn't a flaw in your character. It's your system trying to protect you, trying to alert you, trying to tell you that something is wrong. And it's right. Something is wrong. You're doing work meant for multiple people, and you're doing it alone, and that's legitimately hard. The anger knows that. Maybe it's time you let yourself know it too. ## Keep reading - [Anger at Your Aging Parents Who Won't Be Helped](/article/anger-at-your-aging-parents-who-wont-be-helped) - [Sandwich Generation Anger: The Math Doesn't Work](/article/sandwich-generation-anger-the-math-doesnt-work) - [Anger at Other Parents at the Playground](/article/anger-at-other-parents-at-the-playground) ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on single motherhood and the anger nobody will help with, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.

title: What 'Crazy' Really Meant When He Called You That
dek: *The word "crazy" in an argument isn't a diagnosis. It's a strategic move to end the conversation by making you doubt yourself. Here's what's actually happening when someone deploys that label, and how to recognize when your anger is pointing you toward something true.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. When someone calls you crazy, they're usually trying to erase your legitimate emotional response to something real. The word functions as a control mechanism that shifts blame from their behavior to your perception. Understanding this pattern helps you reclaim your reality and trust your own nervous system.

You know what's wild? The thing we call "going crazy" is often the moment your body finally tells the truth your mind has been avoiding. When someone calls you crazy during a heated moment, they're not making an observation about your mental health. They're making a bid to control the narrative, to frame your emotional clarity as confusion and your boundary as pathology. I've watched this pattern play out in my own practice for years, and the choreography is always the same: you notice something off, you name it, you feel something rise in your chest, and the other person reaches for the nuclear option. "You're being crazy." "You're overreacting." "You're imagining things." The goal isn't accuracy. The goal is to make you second-guess the signal your nervous system just sent. ## The Anatomy of "Crazy" as a Weapon When someone calls you crazy, they're activating a very specific cultural script. Women hear it more than men. People of color hear it when they name racism. Anyone who's been gaslit knows the particular sting of having your reality questioned right when you're most certain of it. The word works because it carries institutional weight. "Crazy" isn't just an insult; it's a diagnosis-shaped cudgel. It borrows authority from psychiatric language without any of the nuance or care. Robert Augustus Masters writes in [To Be a Man - Robert Augustus Masters](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1622039440?tag=spankyspinola-20) about how men in particular learn to weaponize rationality and frame emotional truth as hysteria, a pattern that keeps both parties trapped in destructive loops. In our experience working with people navigating conflict, the "crazy" accusation almost always appears at a predictable moment: right when you've touched something true. You've noticed a pattern. You've named a hurt. You've set a limit. And rather than meet you there, the other person tries to relocate the problem from their behavior to your perception. Think about the physical sensation of being called crazy in the middle of making a point you know is valid. Your throat tightens. Your face gets hot. There's a lurching feeling in your stomach, like the ground just shifted. That's not confirmation that you're wrong. That's your body registering the betrayal of having your reality denied. ## What Your Anger Was Trying to Tell You Here's the thing about anger that most conflict-avoidant people miss: it's information. Peter Levine's work on somatic experiencing shows us that anger is one of the ways the nervous system completes a defensive response. When you feel angry, your body is often trying to push back against something that crossed a line, something that violated your sense of safety or fairness. When someone calls you crazy for being angry, they're asking you to abandon that information. They're asking you to override your own nervous system's assessment of the situation and accept theirs instead. This is crazy-making in the truest sense: being asked to deny what you know to be true creates the exact kind of cognitive dissonance that actually does destabilize people. The anger you felt before someone called you crazy probably had a clear target. You were angry *about* something specific. Maybe they dismissed you. Maybe they lied. Maybe they broke a promise for the third time this month. Anger like that is directional. It points. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches that anger, when met with mindfulness, reveals the hurt beneath it. The person calling you crazy wants you to skip that revelation. They want you to get tangled in defending your sanity instead of staying focused on the thing that hurt you. It's a remarkably effective derailment tactic, and it works because most of us have been taught to fear our own anger more than we fear being mistreated. ## The Gaslighting Gradient Not every use of "crazy" is gaslighting, but they exist on the same spectrum. Gaslighting is the systematic denial of someone's reality in order to gain power over them. Calling someone crazy in an argument is often gaslighting-lite: a quick reality-denial move that doesn't require the long-term commitment of a full gaslighting campaign. You can track this by paying attention to what happens after the accusation. Does the person engage with the content of what you said, or do they focus entirely on the way you said it? Do they address the boundary you set, or do they make your emotional state the entire topic of conversation? Across the articles we've published on this site, one pattern emerges clearly: when someone consistently makes your emotional responses the problem rather than engaging with the situations that provoked those responses, you're dealing with a control issue, not a communication issue. The National Institute of Mental Health recognizes that healthy emotional regulation includes being able to identify, express, and work through difficult feelings. When you visit the NIMH page on emotional regulation, you'll see that suppressing legitimate emotional responses or having them invalidated by others actually contributes to poor mental health outcomes. In other words, being told you're crazy for having feelings can actually make you less stable, not more. ## When You Start to Doubt Yourself The most insidious part of being called crazy is what happens in the hours and days afterward. You start reviewing the footage in your mind. Did you raise your voice? Did you use the wrong word? Were you actually overreacting? The self-interrogation is exhausting, and it's exactly what the accusation was designed to trigger. Your body remembers, though. That's what people forget. Even if your mind starts to waver, your nervous system keeps the score. You might notice you feel tense around this person now. You might find yourself rehearsing conversations before they happen, trying to find the perfectly calm way to express a boundary so you can't be accused of being emotional. That hypervigilance is a symptom. Not of your instability, but of being in a relationship where your reality gets questioned regularly enough that you've learned to doubt it preemptively. One practice that helps: the next time you feel that doubt creeping in, place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Breathe slowly and ask your body, not your mind, what it knows. Your body will tell you if you felt scared, or dismissed, or unseen. It will tell you if the anger that rose up was proportional to the moment. Trust that intelligence. It's older and wiser than the part of you trying to logic your way into being believed. ## The Nervous System Doesn't Lie Here's what I want you to understand on a cellular level: your nervous system evolved over millions of years to keep you safe. It reads threat, connection, safety, and danger faster than your conscious mind can process language. When someone violates your boundaries and then tells you you're crazy for noticing, your body knows the truth. You might feel a clench in your jaw. A heat in your chest. A shakiness in your hands. These aren't signs of instability. They're signs of a nervous system doing exactly what it's supposed to do: alerting you to danger and preparing you to respond. Some people find that supporting the nervous system with magnesium helps during periods of high relational stress. [Pure Encapsulations Potassium Magnesium Citrate (180 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0017I29HG?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help ease the physical tension that accumulates when you're constantly defending your version of reality. But supplements are support, not solution. The real work is learning to trust what your body tells you even when someone else insists you're wrong. Peter Levine's research shows that unresolved activation in the nervous system, when we can't complete a defensive response, gets stored as trauma. Being told you're crazy when you're trying to set a boundary is a kind of thwarted defense. Your system wanted to push back, to establish safety, and instead it got told the threat isn't real. That incomplete cycle has to go somewhere. ## What Healthy Disagreement Actually Looks Like You know what someone who respects you does when you're upset? They might not agree with your interpretation, but they don't question your right to your feelings. They say things like, "I see this differently, but I hear that you're hurt." They stay in the conversation instead of trying to end it by delegitimizing you. Healthy conflict includes raised voices sometimes. It includes strong feelings. What it doesn't include is one person appointing themselves the arbiter of reality and diagnosing the other person's emotional responses as pathological. In relationships where both people feel safe, you can say, "I'm really angry about this" without the other person responding with, "You're being crazy." They might say, "I don't understand why this is such a big deal to you," which opens a conversation. Or, "I'm feeling defensive right now," which names their own response. Both of those keep you in dialogue. "You're crazy" ends dialogue. The difference matters. One approach treats disagreement as a problem to solve together. The other treats your emotional reality as the problem to eliminate. ## Reclaiming Your Reality So what do you do when someone calls you crazy and you're pretty sure you're not? First, you notice it. You mark it as a tactic, not a truth. You might even say out loud, "I'm not interested in debating my sanity. I'm talking about what happened." Second, you check in with your body. Not to see if you're calm enough to be credible, but to see what your nervous system is telling you about this person and this dynamic. If you feel like you're walking on eggshells, if you're constantly moderating your tone, if you've started to doubt your own memory, those are data points. Third, you find people who reflect your reality back to you accurately. This might mean therapy. It might mean trusted friends who've known you long enough to say, "No, you're not crazy, that thing they did was actually messed up." It might mean spending time on [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) or diving into [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) to find nervous-system support that helps you stay grounded in your own knowing. Some people find that herbs traditionally used for mental clarity help during these periods of reality-testing. [Banyan Botanicals Gotu Kola Powder (3.5 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008X9JM68?tag=spankyspinola-20) has a long history in Ayurvedic practice for supporting cognitive function and reducing mental fog. When someone's been messing with your head, sometimes you need that extra support to think clearly. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Reality-Testing Next time someone calls you crazy and you feel that destabilizing wobble, try this: Sit down somewhere quiet. Place both feet flat on the floor. Take three slow breaths, counting to four on the inhale and six on the exhale. Then ask yourself these questions, writing down quick answers: What specifically happened that made me upset? What did I want in that moment? What boundary was crossed? How did my body feel when they called me crazy? Don't edit. Don't make it sound reasonable. Just write what's true. Then read it back. Often, seeing your own words on paper, you'll recognize immediately whether your response was proportional or not. And most of the time, you'll see that it was. You'll see that you were responding to something real, and that "crazy" was just a way to avoid accountability. Keep that piece of paper. If you start to doubt yourself later, read it again. Your reality doesn't change just because someone refused to acknowledge it. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### What if I actually was overreacting? Overreacting is subjective. What feels like an overreaction to someone who hasn't been hurt is often a proportional response to someone who has. Even if your response was bigger than the single incident warranted, anger often accumulates. You might be reacting to the pattern, not just the moment. And even if you genuinely did respond more intensely than the situation called for, that doesn't make you crazy. It makes you human. The question isn't whether your response was perfect; it's whether the other person is willing to understand what prompted it. ### How do I know if I'm actually being gaslit or just in a regular argument? Regular arguments involve two people with different perspectives trying to find common ground or at least understand each other. Gaslighting involves one person systematically denying the other person's reality, memory, or perception. If you find yourself constantly questioning your own memory, if you apologize constantly even when you're not sure what you did wrong, if you feel confused about what's real after most conversations with this person, those are signs of gaslighting. In a regular argument, both people's realities are treated as valid, even if they conflict. ### What if multiple people have called me crazy? This is worth examining, but not in the way you might think. Sometimes multiple people calling you crazy means you're in a social system that punishes certain kinds of truth-telling. Sometimes it means you're expressing yourself in ways that aren't landing clearly, and you might benefit from support in communication. But here's what it doesn't automatically mean: that you're wrong. If you're in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) section of this site, you already know that anger often arises when we're seeing clearly, and that clarity threatens systems built on denial. Trust your body's wisdom, and find practitioners who can help you discern the difference between a nervous system that needs support and a nervous system that's responding accurately to harmful dynamics. ## What Comes After Knowing Once you understand that "crazy" is a tactic, you can't unhear it. Every time someone reaches for that word in the middle of conflict, you'll recognize the move for what it is. This doesn't make it hurt less, but it does make it clearer. Some relationships don't survive that clarity. Some people need you to doubt yourself in order to avoid examining their own behavior. When you stop providing that service, when you stand firmly in your own reality even when it's questioned, you find out who's willing to meet you there and who isn't. The people who love you well will never ask you to choose between your sanity and their comfort. They'll sit with you in the mess of hard conversations. They'll say, "I don't understand, help me understand," instead of, "You're being irrational." They'll recognize that your anger, even when it's directed at them, is information worth listening to. And you'll learn, slowly, to trust the signals your body sends. To know that the heat in your chest when someone crosses a line isn't pathology. It's protection. It's your oldest, wisest self saying: this matters, pay attention, don't let anyone convince you otherwise. That's not crazy. That's the sanest thing in the world. For more on trusting your own discernment and building a practice that honors your full emotional range, visit [the about page](/about) to understand the framework we're building together here. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on what 'crazy' really meant when he called you that, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.

title: Divorce Rage: Honest Accounting
dek: Your rage is not a failure of recovery - it’s the body’s oldest dialect, speaking a truth your diplomacy has been trained to smother. Before you pathologize the fire, consider that it might be the only thing keeping you from disappearing entirely.
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Divorce Rage: Honest Accounting is a place where the body and the spirit meet, and it asks for honest attention rather than control. In our experience, healthy anger is information about a boundary, a value, or a longing that has been crossed or unmet. This piece walks you through what to notice, what to try, and what to leave alone, in plain language and with real care.

--- *Last updated January 5, 2026.* ## The Reframe You Were Never Offered You have been told that anger is a secondary emotion, a mask for hurt. You have been handed breathing exercises and instructed to count backwards from ten. The entire machinery of civilized coping has been aimed at tamping down, reframing, and outgrowing the thing that rises in your chest when you remember the betrayal, the asset spreadsheet, the casual cruelty delivered over coffee. But here is a counterintuitive claim: the rage that hums under your skin is not a problem to solve. It is a form of biological intelligence so precise, so ancient, that overriding it is an act of self-erasure. Your fury is not evidence of how little you have healed. It is a live wire connecting you to a self that refuses to be erased by someone else’s narrative. You have been starving that self with affirmations. Soraya Chemaly, in *Rage Becomes Her*, flips the script entirely: anger is not a disorder of the individual psyche but a rational, clarifying force in the face of structural and interpersonal injustice. She writes that anger is “the emotion that tells us something is wrong,” and for women especially, the suppression of anger is a social enforcement mechanism, not a wellness strategy. When your ex-husband’s lawyer gaslights you in a deposition, when your own family suggests you “move on for the sake of the children,” the clamped jaw and the hot flush climbing your neck are not maladaptive. They are your nervous system registering a boundary violation with more accuracy than your cognitively rearranged thoughts ever could. Your body knows the debt is still outstanding, even after the papers are signed. ## A Sensorimotor Bill of Particulars The body keeps accounts that the mind has been gaslit into ignoring. Pat Ogden, the founder of sensorimotor psychotherapy, observed something crucial about trauma-related anger: it is not just a feeling but a set of incomplete motor plans. The clenched fist that never swung, the tight throat that held back a scream, the legs braced to flee but forced to stay seated across from the mediator - these are “procedural tendencies” that hang, incomplete, in your musculature. Rage, in Ogden’s view, is often the body continuing to prepare for an action that was never allowed to complete. You feel it as a chronic tension in your forearms, a forward-leaning posture you catch yourself in while doing dishes. This is not a personality defect. It is your organism still trying to protect you from a threat that has, in every legal sense, concluded but not, in any physiological sense, ended. One afternoon you notice your right hand has curled into a semi-fist against your thigh for no reason you can name. You uncurl it consciously, only to find it back five minutes later, the nails leaving small crescent moons in the palm. Ogden would call this a “bottom-up” intrusion: the body’s procedural memory overriding the top-down commands of the prefrontal cortex. You have told yourself you forgive him - your therapist nodded solemnly - but your hand is not interested in your forgiveness narrative. It is still holding the steering wheel at the moment you learned about the other apartment, and it has no intention of letting go until the physical impulse is allowed to finish. ## The Charge That Precedes the Story Peter Levine, the developer of somatic experiencing, adds another layer: the rage you feel may be a “sensation of charge” that predates any cognitive storyline. Levine describes how traumatic stress lodges in the nervous system as bound-up survival energy, like a coiled spring. When that energy is triggered - by an email notification, a child’s tone that mirrors his, the smell of the same detergent - you are not simply “overreacting.” Your system is discharging a high-voltage backlog through whatever channel is available. The story you attach to the rage (“he ruined my life,” “I wasted twenty years”) is often a secondary construction, the neocortex hastily providing a plot for a bodily event that is fundamentally pre-verbal. This is why talk therapy alone can feel like rearranging deck chairs on a burning ship: you are discussing the narrative while your biology continues to burn. Try this on for size: you are sitting in a café, and the barista calls out a name that sounds like hers - the other woman’s, the one whose existence you discovered by reading a phone bill. Instantly, your vision narrows, your stomach drops, and a wave of heat breaks across your chest and scalp. Before you form a single thought, your body is already at war. Levine’s work suggests that this reaction is not a sign of being “stuck” in the past; it is the body’s attempt to metabolize a charge that never got a proper discharge sequence. The trembling in your hands? That’s the nervous system doing its job, not a weakness to hide. But you probably gripped your coffee cup harder, squared your shoulders, and swallowed the tremor down. You just aborted your own healing sequence. ## The Inhale That Chokes You Let’s get specific, in the body. Right now, as you read this, notice the architecture of your breath. There’s a good chance you are breathing shallowly into your upper chest, your diaphragm barely moving. That’s the breath of a person who has learned to brace. In the years of the marriage, you learned to suppress a full exhale because a sigh would be interrogated. You learned to hold still to avoid provoking a cold stare. This breathing pattern, once adaptive, has now become your prison. It tells your nervous system, fifty times a minute, that a threat is present. Every shallow sip of air is a telegram to your amygdala: *stay ready*. And you wonder why you feel electric, porous, wired at 3 a.m. Try this now: place one hand on your belly, just below the navel. Without forcing anything, let the next exhale fall out of you as if you’ve just set down a suitcase you didn’t know you were carrying. Let the inhale follow naturally, but focus on the exhale - let it droop into your pelvic floor. Do that three times. What you might notice is not calm, but a tremor. Perhaps a flutter in the solar plexus, a heat blooming in the thighs, or an urge to make a sound. That is the charge Levine and Ogden are talking about. It’s not relaxation. It’s activation trying to complete itself. The rage you’ve been told is toxic might just be a discharge that’s been waiting for a safe biological opening. ## A Five-Minute Practice: The Unswallowed No You can do this in a parked car, in a locked bathroom, on a walk if you don’t mind looking slightly unhinged. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees lightly bent. Bring to mind a specific moment from the divorce - not the whole narrative, just a frozen frame: his hand pushing a document across a table, her name on the cellphone bill, the way he said “you’re being dramatic.” Notice where your body wants to move. There might be a pushing impulse in your arms, a twisting in your torso, a clenching in your jaw. Now, let the arms begin to push against an imaginary wall, or make fists and slowly punch the air, not with force but with attention. Exhale on the push and, here’s the key, let your throat make a sound. Not a word. A guttural “uhh” or “no.” Not shouted, but from the belly. Do this for two minutes. Stop before you exhaust yourself. Stand still, hands on your stomach, and just feel the aftermath. If you feel ridiculous, that’s your socialization talking. The body doesn’t understand ridiculous; it understands movements completed. This isn’t drama; it’s biology with a witness. If the charge feels unbearably high before doing this, a small dose of [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) can take the physical edge off without sedation, allowing the discharge to feel manageable rather than overwhelming. L-theanine, an amino acid found in green tea, increases alpha brain waves associated with calm alertness - it won’t erase the rage, but it can lower the volume just enough that you can stay present with the felt sense without tipping into overwhelm. Think of it as a volume knob, not a mute button. ## The Social Script That Diagnoses You Here is Chemaly’s most incisive point, the one that should be printed on the divorce decree itself: women’s anger is routinely medicalized, while men’s anger is legitimated as righteous. When you fume, you are “bitter” and “vindictive.” When he withholds, stonewalls, or explodes, he is “under stress” or “finally standing up for himself.” You have internalized this script so deeply that you now police your own internal weather. You feel ashamed of your rage fantasies - the imagined courtroom confrontation, the screaming you never did - as if they make you monstrous. But Chemaly asks: who benefits when you turn your justified clarity into a depressive collapse? Who does it serve when your anger goes inward and becomes a diagnosis of generalized anxiety disorder, a prescription for an SSRI that flattens all your edges? The systems that gaslit you during the marriage continue to gaslight you after it. Your rage is an act of refusal to accept their version of events. To pathologize it is to finish their work for them. You might recognize this in the way you minimize your own anger while excusing his. “He’s just a narcissist,” you say, handing him a diagnostic label that lets you off the hook for your own fury. Or “I should have seen it coming,” turning the anger inward as chronic regret. The book [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a lifeline here, not by teaching you to be a calmer woman, but by showing you how anger can be a precise instrument of change. Lerner reframes anger not as a mood disorder but as a signal about the relationship patterns you are embedded in, a signal that deserves to be followed, not sedated. She writes that we often use anger to cover the more vulnerable emotions, but the reverse is also true: we use sadness and self-blame to cover the terrifying clarity of anger. You are not bitter; you are clearing-throated, and that is a threat to a culture that needs you quiet. ## The Ex From Within There’s a more uncomfortable layer. After a marriage of gaslighting, a strange thing happens: you internalize the interrogator. You wake at 2 a.m. not just burning at him, but at yourself, for not leaving sooner, for not being smarter, for still caring. This self-rage is a loyal guard dog that has turned its teeth on your own throat. Ogden might call this a “phobic relationship with one’s own procedural responses.” You fear your own anger so much that you suppress it, only to have it leak out as chronic jaw pain, IBS, or that thing where you burst into tears at an inappropriate moment during a work meeting. The rage hasn’t gone anywhere; it has simply rerouted through your own tissue, becoming a permanent physiological protest you no longer recognize as such. You can feel this on the days when you cannot get out of bed, weighed down by a leaden fatigue that has no physical cause. Levine calls this the freeze response, the nervous system’s last-ditch survival strategy when both fight and flight are impossible. In the marriage, you couldn’t fight - you risked escalation - and you couldn’t flee - the children, the mortgage, the careful life you’d built. So you went into a functional freeze, smiling at school drop-off while your insides played dead. Now, after the divorce, that freeze is dissolving, and the fight energy that was buried beneath it is surfacing chaotically. The depression you feel is not a chemical imbalance; it’s the lid pressing down on a pot that’s finally boiling. Rage and collapse are two sides of the same dysregulated coin. When you learn to titrate the rage - to let it flow in small, manageable bursts like the practice above - the collapse often eases on its own, because the pressure is being vented. ## An Unfinished Lament in the Diaphragm Place your fingers now on the soft hollow where your ribs meet in the center. Press gently. If you feel a dull ache, a knot, or a catch in your breath, you’ve found the somatic storage site of unshed grief and unexpressed rage. This is the celiac plexus, a dense web of nerves the yogis called the “solar plexus” for good reason: it’s a sun of sensation that you’ve been forced to eclipse. For years, you contracted here to prevent sobbing during arguments, to keep from folding at the waist when his words landed. You made your own body a fortress, and now the guards won’t stand down. One quiet morning, try lying on your back with a rolled towel placed directly under this spot - not under the low back, but right at the diaphragm. Let your knees fall open, arms out to the sides. Breathe into the pressure and let your belly soften. You may begin to sob without a story attached. Let the sound move through your unresisting throat. This is not regression; it’s architectural remediation. Some days, the nervous system’s agitation is so pervasive that it won’t settle into any position. On those days, a tool like [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) can offer a temporary scaffold. GABA is the brain’s primary inhibitory neurotransmitter, and in states of chronic hyperarousal, the body’s own supply can become depleted. Taking GABA supplements doesn’t erase the rage, but it can sometimes break the circuit long enough for a practice to take hold. It’s an assist, not a solution; use it when the voltage is too high to tolerate your own skin, and then come back to the body’s own discharge sequence when the edge is off. ## FAQ: Three Questions You’re Actually Asking **Is it normal to fantasize about revenge years after the divorce?** Normal is a meaningless word here. The fantasies - the imagined confrontations, the courtroom scenes, the satisfying retorts you never delivered - are not plans of action but the mind’s way of completing the fight response that was truncated. They are a form of cognitive rehearsal your brain uses to assert agency over an event in which you felt utterly powerless. They become problematic only when you act on them destructively or when you shame yourself for having them. The shame is the real poison; the fantasy is just a movie your survival brain keeps screening to try to wrestle back control. Let it play without investment, and you’ll find it eventually loses its charge. **How do I stop this rage from spilling onto my children?** You won’t stop it by suppressing it - that just delays the inevitable leak into passive aggression, icy withdrawal, or irritability they will internalize as their fault. Instead, model a different relationship with anger. Say aloud, in simple words, “I am feeling a huge wave of anger right now, and it has nothing to do with you. I’m going to go into my room and punch my pillow for two minutes, and then I’ll be back.” You’re not scaring them; you’re demonstrating that anger is a manageable physiological event, not a destructive monster. Children learn emotional regulation by witnessing yours, not by being protected from it entirely. The lie of perfection is the real threat to their well-being. **My anger seems to be making me physically sick - migraines, digestive issues, pain. Is that possible?** Absolutely. The body remembers what the mind tries to forget. Chronic rage that is never discharged will find expression through the autonomic nervous system: migraines from jaw and neck clamping, IBS from a gut locked in sympathetic overdrive, fibromyalgia-like pain from muscles in perpetual bracing. The research of Peter Levine and Pat Ogden has demonstrated that the nervous system, when stuck in a threat-response loop, will eventually break down somatic systems. This does not mean your anger is “toxic”; it means it is somaticized - turned inward because outward expression was forbidden. The cure is not to become serene but to learn to discharge the rage through the body in safe, intentional ways. Your illness is not your enemy; it’s a messenger. ## The Third Position: Neither Endurance nor Explosion You’ve been trapped in a false binary: either you swallow the rage whole and get chronic fatigue, or you unleash it in a messy, retraumatizing blast that proves everyone’s point about your instability. There is a third position, and it’s the one your body has been begging for. It is the position of the alert, resourced witness. In this stance, you can say to the storm inside you, “I feel you. You have every reason to be here. I am not going to let you drive the car, but I am going to pull over and let you run beside the vehicle until you’re spent.” This is not spiritual bypassing; it’s Levine’s “pendulation,” the natural rhythm of moving between activation and settling that all mammals use to complete stress cycles. Gazelles shake after escaping a lion; you clench your jaw and book an extra therapy session to analyze why you’re still not over it. Your body knows how to heal. You’ve just been taught to overrule it with language and self-improvement propaganda. ## Beyond This Room, Your Rage Has Kin Consider this: the rage you feel is not just yours. Chemaly writes about anger as an inheritance, a force passed down through generations of women who bit their tongues, who smiled while being property, who were told their fury was hysteria. When you rage at your ex, you are also raging at the silent grandmother who never got to walk out. You are raging at the culture that tut-tuts at a divorced woman’s intensity while winking at a man’s midlife crisis. Your body is not just processing a personal betrayal; it is carrying a collective charge that has been denied a voice for centuries. This does not make your anger any less authentically yours; it makes it more significant. It connects your kitchen-table fuming to a lineage of women who were never allowed to complete their own defensive actions, and that connection is not a burden - it’s a source of fuel. The next time someone tells you that resentment is like drinking poison and expecting the other person to die, notice that the aphorism is almost always delivered to the person harmed, rarely to the person who did the harming. It is a tidy, shaming device that blames you for your own physiological honesty. Your body is not a poison factory; it’s a justice-seeking organism. The answers you need are not in a gratitude journal. They’re in the unspoken contracts your muscles still hold, the unfinished exhales, the trembling that you stop before it can teach you anything. For a companion mapping of these bodily negotiations, the visual and written work at [the Oracle Lover](https://theoraclelover.com) offers a home for the questions that wellness culture refuses to hold. There’s no summit to reach, no final forgiveness to achieve - only the slow, feral education of letting your anger speak in its own tongue, and trusting that what it has to say might be the only thing that sets you free. ## What the research and the body both say In our experience, the most useful sources on this stay close to the body. For a careful clinical view, see the APA's research on anger and health. If you want to keep going inside this journal, you can read [the about page](/about), take a quiet hour with [more articles in this journal](/articles), or stay in the writing through [our herbal cabinet](/herbs). ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated January 5, 2026. title: When You Realize Your Marriage Made You Smaller
dek: *You learned to call your rage a defect while your ribs tightened and your voice thinned. The real violence wasn't your anger - it was the constant downsizing of a self that knew it was being erased. This is a field guide to feeling the return of your own shape, not through forced forgiveness but through the body’s refusal to stay small.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. When You Realize Your Marriage Made You Smaller is a place where the body and the spirit meet, and it asks for honest attention rather than control. In our experience, healthy anger is information about a boundary, a value, or a longing that has been crossed or unmet. This piece walks you through what to notice, what to try, and what to leave alone, in plain language and with real care.

They called your anger the cancer in the marriage. You believed them, because you were too exhausted not to, because his calm sounded so reasonable while your chest heaved and your fingers tingled with the effort of not throwing something. You became the designated patient of a union that was slowly crushing you, and you swallowed that diagnosis along with a daily dose of self-doubt. *Last updated January 4, 2026.* ## The First Time You Shrank, You Barely Noticed It wasn’t a door slammed in your face. It was a quiet relocation of boundaries, a single sentence from his mouth that carried no malice but all the weight of finality: “Do you really need to take that workshop right now? We have so much going on.” You saw his face - tired, yes, but also faintly annoyed that you’d think of yourself before the household schedule. And you agreed. You said, “You’re right, I can go next year.” The workshop was on your career pivot, the one you’d whispered about at 2 a.m. when he pretended not to hear. Next year never arrived. Neither did the career pivot. This is how women get smaller inside a marriage: not in a single violent incident you can point to for the friends who want a clear villain, but in a thousand micro-negotiations where your fullness is traded for relational quiet. You stop mentioning the book you’re writing because he shifts the topic to his promotion pressures. You learn to not laugh too loudly at parties because he once said your laugh “draws attention.” You fold your body into the passenger seat so completely that one day you realize you haven’t chosen a radio station in five years. When the anger finally surfaces - hot, unwieldy, animal - you shock yourself with it. And then you do what you were trained to do: you name it ugly, and you hide. ## The Body Remembers What the Mind Negotiates Pat Ogden, in her work on sensorimotor psychotherapy, describes how the body encodes survival responses that are older than language. When the fight response is chronically aborted - when the impulse to push back, to shout “stop,” gets overridden by a fear of abandonment or a need for approval - the body doesn’t just forget. It retains a thwarted mobilization, a readiness that turns into muscle armor. You can feel it now if you pause: the weight in your shoulders that isn’t from a desk job, the shallow breath that cuts off at your collarbones because exhaling fully would risk a sound, a sob, a truth. Your spine stiffens in tiny increments as if preparing for an impact that never comes. There is a specific tension pattern you’ve developed, a kind of bodily cringe. Your pelvis tucks under slightly, making you shorter when you stand; your jaw set is a deliberate stillness, not relaxation. You might press your tongue to the roof of your mouth constantly, a nervous habit that dentists notice but that actually comes from holding back words for years. When you lie down at night, your hips feel stuffed with cement. Ogden would say that your body is still trying to complete a defensive movement that was interrupted a decade ago in a kitchen argument where you stopped mid-sentence because he walked out. The anger got stuck in the tendons, and now it hums there, a low-grade inflammation no anti-inflammatory can fully address. ## Your Anger Was Not the Sickness - Premature Peacefulness Was Here’s a counterintuitive claim: the anger you felt was the healthiest thing about you. Not the ragged, desperate, plate-smashing kind - that is often grief in disguise - but the clean, rising heat that knows a boundary has been breached. Robert Augustus Masters draws a sharp distinction between reactive anger, which lashes out from woundedness, and functional anger, which arises to protect and assert. He calls this functional anger our “emotional immune system.” Without access to it, you become what Masters describes as “emotionally flaccid,” a person who talks about letting go before they’ve ever held on, who covers over valid fury with spiritual-sounding placation. You adopted a premature peacefulness. You’d read a few books on mindful communication, perhaps, and you’d start sentences with “I feel” instead of “You always,” which is fine technique except when the “I feel” is used to dilute the rage. You meditated on detachment until you detached from your own instincts. You considered yourself evolved when you didn’t react to the eighth broken promise, but the truth is your nervous system was still screaming, you just stuffed a cloth in its mouth. Masters would say that your anger deserved an altar, not a trash can. It was trying to save your life - the life in which you are not an accessory to someone else’s comfort. The moment you labeled it as the problem, the marriage stopped being a partnership and became a containment unit, with you as the patient. ## The Price of Being Smaller Shows Up in Bone and Breath The downsizing isn’t just psychological. Your lungs actually shrink their capacity when you chronically brace your ribs against emotional pain, a phenomenon that gets misread as a sedentary lifestyle. Your digestion rebels because the enteric nervous system - the gut brain - knows the truth even when your prefrontal cortex spins stories about how “he’s really a good man.” You develop irritable bowel syndrome or unexplained bloating, and doctors hand you fiber supplements while missing the years of swallowed ultimatums. Your voice changes. It’s the first thing I notice when a client talks about marital erosion: the pitch has gone up, the resonance flattened, the words come out softer as if the air pressure behind them has been regulated to avoid disturbance. You’ve learned to sound agreeable so thoroughly that your laryngeal muscles have literally shortened. Sometimes you clear your throat in the middle of sentences, a nervous tic that inserts a pause for permission. The body remembers every time you were told your tone was “aggressive,” and now you speak as if your own voice is an intruder. Meanwhile, the suppressed fury doesn’t disappear; it condenses into symptoms. It can settle in your temporomandibular joint, giving you jaw clicks and morning headaches that no mouthguard can fix because the clenching happens all day, during every strained family dinner. Or it moves downward, pooling as a chronic burn in your stomach lining. If your anger runs hot - the kind that makes your ears flame and your skin prickle - you might be experiencing what Ayurvedic medicine calls a pitta imbalance, a metabolic fire that has no proper channel. Sometimes the jaw unclenches a degree when magnesium floods the nerve endings - find a form like [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20) that the body can actually use, the glycinate kind that crosses the blood-brain barrier and tells your muscles they can finally stop guarding. And when the stomach churns with resentment that you’ve been naming as indigestion, an Ayurvedic formula like [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) can cool the inner heat enough that you might feel the grief underneath - which is what you were actually supposed to process ten years ago. ## Sensation: The Night You Realized Your Hip Was a Vault It’s 3:14 a.m. and you’re awake again, pinned by a sharp ache deep in your left hip flexor. You’ve tried stretching, foam rolling, and a yoga video you hate. Nothing works because the hip isn’t tight in a muscular sense - it’s holding. If you breathe into it and apply gentle pressure with your palm, you might feel a sudden wave of heat, almost like a cramp releasing, and for a second you’re not in your bedroom but back in the passenger seat of the car, the night he told you a move across the country was already decided. You hadn’t even opened your mouth to object before your hip seized, a silent pre-verbal “no” that twisted your pelvis into protect mode. That’s the sensorimotor loop Ogden writes about: the body encodes the procedural memory of that defeat, and until you allow a completion of the blocked action - maybe by standing, rooting your feet, and slowly pushing your hands forward as if to say “I will not be moved” - the hip will keep aching. It’s not psychosomatic. It’s a repressed impulse living in your connective tissue, and it will outlast your conscious amnesia. ## What Thich Nhat Hanh Knew About Anger as Suffering The dominant message you received - from wellness culture, from certain therapy modalities, from his wounded defense - was that anger is destructive and bad, something to be transformed into forgiveness as quickly as possible. Thich Nhat Hanh offered a radically different lens. He taught that anger is not your enemy; it is a suffering that needs to be cradled. He used the image of a mother holding a crying baby, saying that when you are angry, you must hold that anger with the same tender awareness. The problem, he insisted, is not the anger but the lack of mindfulness around it - the way we either let it explode or shove it into the basement. When you hid your anger to keep the marriage intact, you were not practicing non-attachment; you were practicing self-abandonment. Hanh’s approach was not to “let go” of anger prematurely but to breathe with it, acknowledge it as a painful signal, and ask it what it needs. Imagine if someone had told you that during the early years: that the tight, burning knot in your gut was a crying baby, not a snake to be killed. You would have sat down, placed a hand there, and maybe whispered, “I see you. I’m listening.” Instead, you got a husband who called it your “temper problem” and a therapist who asked, “What fear is underneath that anger?” as if the anger itself was a cover story. The anger was the story. Your marriage was suffocating you, and your body knew it long before your mind could form the words. On mornings when the residue of old fights tightens your throat, a spoon of [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) can lubricate the path from gut to voice - ghee, in Ayurveda, is a balm for vata derangement, the dry erratic energy that comes from years of willed silence. It’s not a spiritual bypass; it’s a physical support so you can finally speak without a strangled catch. ## A Practice: Noticing Without Apology (Under Five Minutes) Sit somewhere you won’t be interrupted - a closet if necessary. Place both feet flat on the ground. Let your hands rest on your thighs, palms up, a posture of receptiveness not defense. Close your eyes and bring your attention to the place in your body where anger tends to lodge. For many, it’s the space between the shoulder blades or the right side of the jaw. For you, it might be the solar plexus, that hot hollow. Don’t try to relax it. Instead, breathe into that area as if you’re blowing on a small ember - not to put it out, but to give it oxygen. Inhale slowly through your nose, imagining the breath curving into that tight spot. On the exhale, let out a soundless internal phrase, something like: “You have every right to be here.” Not “It’s okay.” That’s different. “You have every right.” Do this for ten breaths. If a memory or image surfaces - his face, a specific meal where he dismissed your job, the way he turns his back when you cry - don’t follow it into a narrative. Just note it and return your attention to the body sensation. After ten breaths, if you can, press the palm of your hand gently against that area and sustain the contact for another minute while allowing your jaw to unclench just 10%. That’s it. This practice isn’t about resolving the anger; it’s about ending your war with it. Do it daily for a week, and you may find that your body no longer needs to send pain signals to get your attention. ## Questions You Ask When the Silence Breaks **Is it too late to find my anger again after all these years?** No. Anger is not a finite resource that dissipates. It is a biological potential stored in your nervous system and visceral memories. You’ve simply locked it away. Returning to it requires safety - you may need to first create that safety by leaving the room, speaking to a therapist who understands somatics, or telling a trusted friend “I am angry” without softening it. When you do, the anger will surface. It might feel like a geyser at first, unrefined and terrifyingly large. That’s normal. You’re not “becoming” angry; you’re finally letting the pressure equalize. **What if expressing my anger destroys whatever is left of the marriage?** The marriage, as it currently exists, is already a wreckage if you cannot express your authentic state. The question isn’t whether the marriage will survive your anger; it’s whether you can survive one more year without its expression. Masters would say that a relationship that cannot withstand clean anger is already dead, held together only by your self-suppression. You are not obligated to preserve a union at the cost of your life force. When you start speaking truthfully, the partnership will either reconfigure into something healthier or collapse. Both outcomes are better than the slow erasure you’re in now. **I don’t even feel angry - just numb and sad. Is that the same thing?** Numbness is often anger’s winter coat. Many women in long-term minimizing marriages have been so thoroughly trained to override their fight response that the anger has gone underground. It emerges as a heavy, directionless depression, or as somatic symptoms like fatigue and brain fog. If you start the practice above and feel nothing physical, that’s information: your dissociation is so deep that you need to first re-establish body awareness. A somatic therapist can help. The sadness, too, is likely anger turned inward - a mourning for the self you abandoned. As Hanh would say, you hold that sadness too, but you need to know it’s protecting a fiercer truth underneath. ## The Door Was Never Locked One morning, not long from now, you might find yourself standing in the kitchen, alone with a cup of coffee, and there will be a heat rising in your chest that you don’t immediately name as panic. You’ll realize, for the first time, that you can take up the full width of the counter without someone moving you aside. You might spread your elbows, widen your stance, and feel the floor against your soles as if you just landed there. It will be a small, almost ridiculous gesture, but your nervous system will register it as a revolution. The jaw will loosen a fraction. The hip will settle. This isn’t about leaving or staying. It’s about the body’s refusal to continue at half capacity, its persistent memory of a shape that was once yours. Your anger didn’t come to wreck you; it came to remind you that you were never meant to fold into a drawer. If you want to trace that reminder further, without the sanitized language of self-help, there’s a place at [the Oracle Lover](https://theoraclelover.com) where we refuse to pathologize what is holy in you. But for now, just stand there. Let the kitchen be quiet. Notice the space you now allow your own arms, the unclipped breath, the faint humming that is not dissatisfaction but the sound of size returning. ## What the research and the body both say In our experience, the most useful sources on this stay close to the body. For a careful clinical view, see the APA's research on anger and health. If you want to keep going inside this journal, you can read [the about page](/about), take a quiet hour with [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), or stay in the writing through [the assessments hub](/assessments). ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated January 4, 2026. In our work on when you realize your marriage made you smaller, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
